title: Evangelions manga how to adapt something by losing and gaining
display_title: Evangelions manga how to adapt something by losing and gaining
wikitext:
[]: Evangelion's manga - how to adapt something by losing and gaining             Quote Post by BernardoCairo » Tue Dec 29, 2020 11:46 pm When adapting an already existing work to another media, you will always be missing out on some of the things that merged to create that product's identity. Evangelion is no different. As all of you may know, even though the manga came out a little bit earlier than the anime (because of some delay issues whitin Gainax's production window), the latter is considered to be the original series. That's because the story of NGE was conceptualized for it first. Besides that, the anime quickly caught up with the manga and finished well before (around 17 years if you consider The End of Evangelion). Thus, most of the manga can be seen as an adaptation. Basically, Sadamoto was tasked with creating his own take on a very popular and influential story. So, how it turned out to be? In my opinion, pretty good. That's because he understood how to work with the new tools he had at hand. He knew where to lose and gain with it. A good example of something that he lost while writing the manga was Asuka's break down at volume nine. He did not had the sounds, flashy visuals or Anno's direction to help him convey that segment (which in one of the most powerful and horrific in the series). But, by using this opportunity to explore Asuka's past a bit further, I think he managed to buit an interesting scenario in his own right. Not necessarily better than what we saw in the anime, but amusing in it's own way. A good example of something that he gained while working with the manga can be seen in volume four. At first glance it seems like he completely ditch episode eight from the mix (which is one of the best in the series). But, what he did instead was merge it with episode nine. For me, that was a genius move. When you are working with a manga, you certainly have way less time to develop your characters. Therefore, focusing on the big picture is way more important than wasting your resources with minor details. By centering the narrative in only one angel, Sadamoto was able to gain more time to explore Shinji and Asuka's relationship (the main point of that whole "arc"). Their training, first meeting and even basic interactions felt way more natural because of it. Giving Asuka a major win from the start (against the sixth angel) also helped to consolidate her character as both a great pilot and someone who seeks the attention of others all the time (evident by the way she approached Kaji, boasting for defeating the angel only by herself). In my opinion, this part of the manga came out even better than its anime counterpart. In the end, all of this was only possible because Sadamoto had a lot of creative freedom and, therefore, could take the narrative anywhere he wanted (following the events of the original series just as a guideline). If this was an adaptation that tried to be as faithful as possible, much would be cut and the manga would end up as a simple inferior version of something that already exists. In conclusion, being flexible and knowing how to deal with the materials you are working with is very important.

[]: Re: Evangelion's manga - how to adapt something by losing and gaining             Quote Post by Javi2541997 » Wed Jan 20, 2021 2:53 am Hello there, Thank you for clarifying this. I was looking since the past year for a good review of NGE Manga because I want to give it a chance. I think your review was the best I have read. It gives me good reasons to buy right now manga and starting something new and interesting from EVA that I never knew about. When I'd buy it I will give you a review! Thanks for this recommendation.

[]: Re: Evangelion's manga - how to adapt something by losing and gaining             Quote Post by BernardoCairo » Wed Jan 20, 2021 10:17 am Actually, thank you for reading it! I thought that this text of mine would be completely lost here on the forum forever hahaha! By the way, it's nice to see that you are going after the manga! But, before you do, just a piece of advice. Do not expect this to be the same as the original series! It is different, it is fast and it cut corners! But, if you accept it for what it is I'm sure you will find it to be fun! There are some really cool surprises in here!
